title: STANDARD FOR QUICK FROZEN FISH FILLETS CODEX STAN 190 -1995

sections.section: STANDARD FOR QUICK FROZEN FISH FILLETS CODEX STAN 190 -1995
sections.summary: Document title only: Codex Standard for Quick Frozen Fish Fillets (CODEX STAN 190-1995).

sections.section: Adopted in 1995. Revised in 2017. Amended in 2011, 2013, 2014.
sections.summary: Administrative note: adoption and amendment history (adopted 1995; revised 2017; amended 2011, 2013, 2014).

Scope: the standard applies to quick frozen fish fillets intended for direct consumption without further processing and does not apply to products labeled as for further processing or other industrial purposes.

Description overview: defines quick frozen fillets by product and processing requirements (rapid freezing through the crystallization range and attainment of −18°C or colder at the thermal centre, maintaining deep‑frozen storage to preserve quality), permits controlled repacking with re‑freezing, and allows any presentation if it meets the standard and is adequately labelled, including boneless fillets after pinbone removal.

Product definition: quick frozen fillets are irregularly sized/ shaped slices of the same fish species removed by cuts parallel to the backbone and prepared consistent with the process definitions in Section 2.2 for human consumption.

Process definition: fillets must be quick frozen so the temperature passes the maximum crystallization range rapidly and reaches −18°C or colder at the thermal centre after stabilization, be kept deep frozen to maintain quality, be processed/packaged to minimize dehydration and oxidation, and may be repacked under controlled conditions followed by reapplication of the quick freezing process.

Presentation: any product presentation is permitted if it meets the standard and is clearly described on the label to avoid consumer confusion, and fillets may be sold boneless provided pinbones have been removed.

Sets essential composition and quality factors for quick frozen fish fillets: fish must be sound and fit to be sold fresh; glazing water must be potable or clean sea-water per WHO microbiological standards; all other ingredients must be food-grade and meet Codex standards; histamine limit of 10 mg/100 g (average) applies to specified fish families; final product conformity is determined by tests in Section 7 and lot inspection per Section 9 against provisions in Section 8.
Requires quick frozen fish fillets to be prepared from sound fish of a quality fit to be sold fresh for human consumption.

If glazed, glazing water or glazing solutions must be potable (meeting WHO "International Guidelines for Drinking Water Quality") or clean sea-water that meets the same microbiological standards and is free from objectionable substances.
All other ingredients used in the product must be food-grade and conform to applicable Codex standards.

Limits histamine to not more than 10 mg/100 g (based on the average of the sample unit tested) and states this limit applies only to species of the Clupeidae, Scombridae, Scombresocidae, Pomatomidae and Coryphaenedae families.
Requires products to meet the standard when lots examined per Section 9 comply with provisions in Section 8 and specifies that examinations shall use the methods given in Section 7.

Permits use of antioxidants and humectants listed in Tables 1 and 2 of the General Standard for Food Additives (CODEX STAN 192-1995) for food category 09.2.1 (frozen fish, fillets and related products) and its parent categories.

Recommends preparation and handling per relevant Codex hygiene codes, requires compliance with any Codex microbiological criteria, and mandates that when tested the product be free from hazardous microorganisms/substances, contain no more than 20 mg/100 g histamine for specified fish families, and contain no other hazardous substances in amounts contrary to Codex standards.

Adds specific labelling provisions to the General Standard: requires prescribed naming and presentation declarations, a "quick frozen" claim (with a "frozen" exception where customary), storage/quality maintenance statements, and a sea-water glazing statement where applicable, plus rules on net contents, storage temperature and labelling of non-retail containers (detailed in subsections 6.1–6.4).

Requires the product name to be declared as "... fillets" or "fillets of ...", to include a proximate presentation reference to avoid misleading consumers, to state "quick frozen" (except where "frozen" is customary under subsection 2.2), to include a quality-maintaining storage statement, and to indicate if glazed with sea-water.
States that for glazed products the declared net contents must exclude the weight of the glaze.
Requires the label to state that the product shall be stored at a temperature of -18°C or colder.

Permits required labelling information to be on the non-retail container or accompanying documents, but mandates that product name, lot identification, manufacturer/packer name and address, and storage instructions appear on the container (lot identification and name/address may be replaced by an identification mark if clearly linked to accompanying documents).

Specifies sampling plans (AQL 6.5) and sample unit definitions, requires sensory and physical exams by trained personnel per cited procedures, sets methods for net-weight determination (frozen state and a glaze-removal method), prescribes a Type 1 candling method for parasite detection, references AOAC methods for gelatinous condition, provides cooking procedures for sensory tests, and specifies histamine analytic performance criteria (e.g., ML 10 mg/100 g average; LOD 1; LOQ 2; RSDR 16%; recovery 90–107) and acceptable methods (e.g., AOAC 977.13, NMKL methods).

Requires lot sampling for product examination per an appropriate sampling plan with an AQL of 6.5 (sample unit = primary container or at least 1 kg for individually quick frozen products) and net-weight sampling per Codex criteria.

States that sensory and physical examinations must be performed by trained personnel following procedures in Sections 7.3–7.6, Annex A and the Guidelines for Sensory Evaluation of Fish and Shellfish in Laboratories (CAC/GL 31-1999).
Mandates that the net weight (exclusive of packaging) of each sample unit representing a lot be determined in the frozen state.

sections.section: 7.3.1 Determination of Net Weight of Products Covered by Glaze
sections.summary: Describes glaze removal for net-weight determination: thaw package briefly, spray contents with cold water and agitate to remove visible ice glaze, blot off adhering water with paper towel, then weigh the product in a tared pan.

sections.section: 7.4 Procedure for the Detection of Parasites (Type 1 Method) in skinless fillets
sections.summary: Prescribes a non-destructive Type 1 parasite-detection method for skinless fillets: examine the entire thawed sample unit on a 5 mm thick acrylic sheet (45% translucency) candled with a 1500 lux light positioned 30 cm above the sheet.
Specifies determination of gelatinous condition by the referenced AOAC methods (Moisture in Meat and Meat Products, procedures 983.18 and 950.46).

Prescribes several cooking procedures for the product for sample evaluation — target internal temperature 65–70°C, avoid overcooking, and gives specific methods: baking (wrapped on a pan), steaming (wrapped on a rack over boiling water), boil‑in‑bag (sealed in boilable pouch), and microwave (in microwave‑safe container; check plastics for odour), with exact times to be determined by prior experimentation.

Sets method performance criteria for histamine determination for an ML of 10 mg/100 g (applicable range ~8–12 mg/100 g), requiring LOD ≈1 mg/100 g, LOQ ≈2 mg/100 g, RSDR 16%, recoveries 90–107%, and lists acceptable reference methods (e.g., AOAC 977.13, NMKL methods).

Heading introducing criteria for when a sample unit is considered 'defective' and listing specific defect types (dehydration, foreign matter, parasites, bones, odour/flavour, flesh abnormalities) with definitions and thresholds in subsequent subsections.

Defines dehydration as excessive moisture loss affecting more than 10% of the sample unit's surface (or exceeding specified defect area thresholds by pack size: ≤200 g → ≥25 cm2, 201–500 g → ≥50 cm2, 501–5000 g → ≥150 cm2) visible as white/yellow abnormalities that penetrate below the surface and cannot be removed without unduly affecting appearance.

Defines foreign matter as any non‑fish material in the sample unit that does not pose a health threat but is readily recognizable (without magnification) or detectable by methods including magnification and indicates non‑compliance with good manufacturing and sanitation practices.

Considers a sample unit defective for parasites when two or more parasites per kg are detected (by the method in Section 7.4) with either a capsular diameter >3 mm or, if not encapsulated, a length >10 mm.

For packs designated boneless, deems defective if more than one bone per kg is ≥10 mm in length or ≥1 mm in diameter, while bones ≤5 mm length are not defects if diameter ≤2 mm; the bone 'foot' is disregarded if width ≤2 mm or removable with a fingernail.
Declares a sample unit defective if it has persistent, distinct objectionable odours or flavours characteristic of decomposition, rancidity or feed.
Defines flesh abnormalities as excessive gelatinous flesh with any individual fillet >86% moisture, or pasty texture from parasitic infestation affecting more than 5% of the sample unit by weight.

Specifies lot acceptance criteria: (i) number of defectives per Section 8 must not exceed the acceptance number of a sampling plan with AQL 6.5; (ii) average net contents of examined containers must be ≥ declared weight with no unreasonable shortages; and (iii) Food Additives, Hygiene and Labelling requirements in Sections 4–6 must be met.

Lists sensory and physical examination procedures: determine net weight (de‑glaze if needed), measure dehydration area and percent affected, thaw and inspect each fillet for foreign matter/parasites/bones/odour/flesh defects, and where odour or gelatinous condition is inconclusive in the raw state, confirm by cooking per Section 7.6 or by moisture determination per Section 7.5 (use 7.5 if cooking is inconclusive).
Annex A — heading only; no substantive content provided.
